Q1: I tried to keep things organized, but honestly, my routine was pretty loose this semester. I'd usually set aside time in the evenings to go through course materials, maybe three or four hours at a stretch. Being a visual learner, I'd spend a lot of time watching lecture videos and creating my own diagrams and notes. Some weeks I was more consistent than others, but I aimed for around 22 hours total across the week.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I'd have good intentions to study at regular times, but life would get in the way. When deadlines approached, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks, I'd sometimes let things slide. It wasn't the most disciplined approach, but it's how I managed to get through with everything else going on.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if new materials had been posted or seeing if there were any announcements about assignments. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch a video I'd been putting off. If I got a notification about a deadline coming up, that would definitely get me to log in. It wasn't always because I wanted to—sometimes it was more out of obligation.
Q4: For a while, yeah, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine along with coffee. I'd scroll through to see what was new, check discussion boards briefly. But that habit faded as the semester went on and I started feeling more drained. The routine stuck for maybe the first month or two, then became more sporadic.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me, hands down. I watched almost three hours worth because I really need to see concepts visualized to understand them. Being able to pause, rewind, and take screenshots for my notes made a huge difference. The visual materials like diagrams and infographics also helped me connect ideas better than just reading text.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums most of the time—only posted twice the whole semester. They felt like busywork to me, and I never got much out of reading other people's posts. Some of the reading materials I'd skim or skip entirely if I felt the videos covered the same content. If something didn't seem immediately useful for assignments, I'd deprioritize it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. If I watched a video and immediately understood how it would help me complete my work, that felt valuable. Also, anything that was visually engaging kept my attention better. If something felt like it was just padding the course, I'd lose interest fast.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, honestly. With my motivation being pretty low this semester, I focused on what was required and let the optional stuff go. Required activities had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them or my grade would suffer. Optional ones just didn't create that same urgency, even if they might have been helpful.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the lecture slides and PDFs early on and studied from those on my tablet without being connected. I also used YouTube for supplementary explanations when the course materials weren't clicking. My handwritten notes from the videos were probably my main study tool, and none of that shows up in any system.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be reviewing my notes or working through problems on paper for hours, and the LMS would show nothing. I'd download a video once, then rewatch it multiple times offline while taking notes. Or I'd be studying with a classmate, going over concepts together, but since we weren't using the LMS, there'd be no trace of that work.
Q11: Yeah, that was pretty common for me. I'd access a lecture video or download the slides once, then study from those files repeatedly over the following days. My internet is good, but I preferred having materials saved locally so I could study without distractions. One download could turn into hours of offline review.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. I was feeling pretty sad and unmotivated, and it became difficult to make myself engage with anything. The stress was building up, and even though I'm usually pretty resilient, my mood just tanked. I'd open the LMS and just stare at it without really absorbing anything. That period was rough.
Q13: Definitely. When I was stressed or fatigued, I'd log into the LMS but not really do anything meaningful. I'd click around, maybe start a video, but my mind would be elsewhere. Competing responsibilities—work stuff, personal issues—meant the course sometimes had to take a backseat. My attendance dropped because I just couldn't keep up with everything.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have a video playing but be scrolling on my phone or just zoning out. Sometimes I'd open readings and skim through them without actually processing the information. It felt like going through the motions. The LMS would register that I accessed something, but my brain wasn't really there.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. My motivation was just so low that I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. The bigger assignments especially—I'd avoid them because they felt overwhelming. I knew it was hurting my completion rate, but I couldn't seem to push through it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving sometimes. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates. Without that external pressure, I'd drift. It's not the healthiest way to work, but it's how I survived this semester.
Q17: When I got feedback on assignments, it did motivate me a bit—especially when it was positive. My assignment scores were pretty good when I actually submitted, so that reinforced that I could do the work. But feedback came slowly sometimes, and by then I'd already moved on mentally. It helped, but not as much as it could have.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it didn't really push me to stay consistently engaged. Having six online courses meant a lot of self-direction was required, and that's hard when motivation is low. The weekly modules gave some rhythm, but I often fell behind and had to catch up in bursts. More structured check-ins might have helped me stay on track.
Q19: The video content being well-produced made engagement easier since I'm a visual learner. But the sheer amount of content across all my courses made it harder to keep up. Discussion requirements felt forced and didn't add much for me. If there had been more interactive visual elements or shorter, focused modules, I think I would have engaged more consistently.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out but struggling to produce actual work. Maybe they're watching videos, reading materials, searching for understanding, but when it comes to assignments, something blocks them—confusion, anxiety, perfectionism. Activity doesn't always translate to output, especially when you're not in a good headspace.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. My exam score wasn't great, but I passed, and my assignment scores were solid when I submitted. A lot of my real learning happened offline with downloaded materials and my own notes. Someone looking at my login frequency might think I wasn't trying, but I was putting in work—just not always where it could be tracked.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you're clicking on, how long you spend on videos. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through motions. They miss offline study, group work, the mental and emotional state you're in. Numbers don't capture understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just logging in or completing tasks—it's being present and curious. For me, engagement looks like creating notes that help me visualize concepts, asking myself questions, connecting ideas. It's an internal state that external metrics can't fully capture.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally this semester, or how that sadness affected everything. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with downloaded materials, or the peer support that helped me push through. It wouldn't capture that I actually understood the material pretty well when I engaged with it—my assignment scores show that. The data tells a partial story at best.
